question: Explain the mechanism by which chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy mediates tumor cell lysis in hematologic malignancies, including the intracellular signaling cascades involved and the role of costimulatory domains.
answer:
CAR T-cell therapy reprograms a patient’s autologous T lymphocytes to recognize and destroy malignant cells via non-major histocompatibility complex–restricted antigen targeting. The ectodomain comprises a single-chain variable fragment (scFv) derived from a monoclonal antibody that binds a lineage-restricted tumor antigen (e.g., CD19 on B-cell malignancies). This ectodomain is fused via a transmembrane hinge (CD8 or CD28) to one or more intracellular signaling domains.

First-generation CARs contained only the CD3ζ immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motif (ITAM), which, upon antigen engagement, recruits the Src-family kinase Lck. Lck phosphorylates CD3ζ on three conserved ITAMs, creating docking sites for the Syk-family kinase ZAP-70. ZAP-70 phosphorylation of adaptor LAT and scaffold SLP-76 nucleates the LAT signalosome, activating phospholipase C-γ1 (PLC-γ1). PLC-γ1 cleaves PIP₂ into IP₃ and DAG, raising intracellular Ca²⁺ and activating protein kinase C-θ (PKC-θ), which translocates to the immunological synapse. The Ca²⁺–calcineurin–NFAT and DAG–RasGRP–Ras–MAPK pathways synergistically drive cytokine secretion (IFN-γ, TNF-α) and cytolytic granule (perforin, granzyme B) polarization.

Second- and third-generation CARs incorporate costimulatory domains (CD28, 4-1BB, ICOS) cloned in tandem with CD3ζ. CD28 contains a PYAP motif that binds PI3K p85 and ITK, amplifying PLC-γ1 activation and Akt-mTOR signaling, thereby enhancing T-cell proliferation and survival. 4-1BB (CD137), a TNF receptor superfamily member, recruits TRAF1/2 and activates the non-canonical NF-κB pathway via MAP3K14 (NIK), conferring increased mitochondrial biogenesis and resistance to exhaustion. These modifications yield CAR T cells with sustained metabolic fitness and enhanced persistence in vivo.

Clinical relevance is highlighted by tisagenlecleucel (CD19-28ζ CAR) and axicabtagene ciloleucel (CD19-28ζ CAR) approvals for relapsed/refractory B-ALL and large B-cell lymphoma, respectively, with objective response rates exceeding 80% yet cytokine-release syndrome and neurotoxicity remain dose-limiting toxicities linked to excessive cytokine flux. Ongoing trials (NCT04501757) evaluate universal CAR T constructs using CRISPR-edited TRAC locus to ablate endogenous TCR and insert CD19 CAR under the control of synthetic Notch (synNotch) circuits to enable tumor-restricted activation.

Summary: CAR T-cell lysis is initiated by antigen-driven phosphorylation cascades through CD3ζ ITAMs and ZAP-70, amplified by costimulatory domains (CD28, 4-1BB) that activate PI3K-Akt, NF-κB, and metabolic programs, culminating in perforin/granzyme-mediated tumor apoptosis while balancing therapeutic efficacy and toxicity in hematologic cancers.
